heroOverview: Pyoderma is a bacterial infection of the skin characterised by pus-forming inflammation; the term encompasses primary infections arising in previously normal skin and secondary infections superimposed on pre-existing skin disease. The two dominant organisms are Staphylococcus aureus and Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes), both of which colonise healthy skin and exploit breaks in the epidermal barrier to establish infection. Superficial pyodermas — impetigo, folliculitis, and furuncles — are among the most common infectious skin diseases worldwide, with impetigo alone affecting an estimated 140 million people at any given time according to WHO data. Deeper forms including cellulitis and skin abscesses are a leading cause of emergency department visits in high-income countries. Most superficial pyodermas respond to topical or short-course oral antibiotics, while deeper or recurrent infections require culture-guided systemic therapy and investigation of underlying predisposing conditions.

keyStats.prevalence: Impetigo alone affects approximately 140 million people worldwide at any time (WHO); cellulitis and skin abscesses account for over 14 million emergency visits annually in the United States
keyStats.demographics: Superficial forms peak in children aged 2-6 years; deeper pyodermas are more common in adults with diabetes, obesity, or skin barrier disruption
keyStats.avgAge: Impetigo most common in children under 6; cellulitis and abscesses peak in adults aged 45-65
keyStats.globalCases: Pyoderma is the most common skin infection worldwide, ranking as one of the top three most prevalent skin diseases in children in low-income countries

definition: Pyoderma (ICD-10: L08.0) is a collective term for bacterial skin infections characterised by the production of pus (pyogenic infection), caused predominantly by Staphylococcus aureus, Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes), or a mixture of both. The term covers a spectrum from superficial infections confined to the epidermis and hair follicles — impetigo, folliculitis, and furunculosis — to deeper infections of the dermis, subcutaneous tissue, and beyond, including cellulitis and skin abscesses. Pyodermas are classified as primary when they arise in intact or minimally damaged skin, and secondary when they develop as a complication of an underlying dermatological condition such as atopic dermatitis, scabies, or varicella. The condition falls under dermatology and, when complicated by deeper infection or systemic illness, under infectious disease. In tropical and subtropical regions and in low-income settings, pyoderma is overwhelmingly common, particularly in children, and untreated streptococcal pyoderma is a recognised antecedent of acute post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. In high-income settings, community-acquired methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (CA-MRSA) has become an important cause of recurrent and difficult-to-treat pyoderma, particularly of skin abscesses.

typesClassification.description: Superficial epidermal infection; non-bullous impetigo causes honey-coloured crusted lesions, most often around the nose and mouth in children; bullous impetigo produces fluid-filled blisters caused by staphylococcal exfoliative toxins.

Infection of the hair follicle producing small pus-filled papules or pustules at the follicle opening; most often caused by S. aureus; superficial folliculitis (Bockhart's impetigo) is common in areas of shaving or moisture.

A furuncle is a deep, painful abscess of a single hair follicle and surrounding tissue; a carbuncle is a coalescence of multiple follicular abscesses, typically on the back of the neck, producing a large, deep, draining lesion.

Spreading bacterial infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue presenting as a warm, red, swollen, tender area without a defined abscess cavity; most often caused by beta-haemolytic streptococci or S. aureus.

A deeper ulcerative form of impetigo where infection penetrates the full epidermis to create a punched-out ulcer covered by a thick adherent crust; associated with neglected impetigo or immunosuppression.

primarySymptoms: Erythema — localised redness of the affected skin area, typically the earliest visible change as bacteria proliferate in the dermis and trigger an inflammatory response.
Oedema and swelling — accumulation of inflammatory fluid in the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, giving the affected skin a raised, turgid appearance.
Warmth — the affected area feels distinctly warmer than surrounding skin due to increased blood flow driven by cytokine-mediated vasodilation.
Tenderness and pain — the infected skin is painful to touch and may throb or ache at rest; deep folliculitis and furuncles produce particularly intense localised pain.
Pus or purulent discharge — the hallmark of pyoderma; pus may point through the skin surface (pustule), drain from a ruptured follicle, or be expressed from an abscess cavity.
Honey-coloured crusting — the classic superficial sign of impetigo, formed by dried serous and purulent exudate over an eroded or vesicular lesion.
Blisters or vesicles — bullous impetigo produces fragile fluid-filled blisters containing cloudy pus, which rupture to leave a moist, raw base.
Follicular papules and pustules — tiny pus-filled spots at hair follicle openings across a shaved or occluded area indicate folliculitis.
Lymphangitis — a red streak tracking up the limb toward the nearest lymph node group signals bacterial spread along lymphatic channels and demands prompt treatment.
Systemic signs — fever, chills, malaise, and a raised pulse develop when infection deepens or bacteria enter the bloodstream, particularly in vulnerable patients.
earlyWarningSigns: A small cluster of itchy vesicles or pustules appearing around the nose or mouth of a child — the earliest sign of non-bullous impetigo.
A painful red lump at a hair follicle that is enlarging, suggesting a developing furuncle before it fully points.
Creeping redness at the margin of a wound or skin break that is spreading despite cleaning.
Increased warmth and swelling around a cut or insect bite that does not settle within 24-48 hours.
emergencySigns: Rapidly spreading redness with severe pain, swelling, and skin discolouration (purplish or grey) that moves faster than expected — possible necrotising fasciitis, a surgical emergency.
Fever above 38.5°C, confusion, rapid heart rate, or very low blood pressure alongside a skin infection — signs of sepsis requiring immediate hospital care.
A painful skin area that feels numb or has lost sensation — this combination of pain and anaesthesia is characteristic of necrotising fasciitis.
A young child with rapidly spreading facial swelling, high fever, and impetigo — risk of bacteraemia and complications such as orbital cellulitis.
Any deep infection in a person with poorly controlled diabetes, on immunosuppressive therapy, or with peripheral vascular disease, who may deteriorate rapidly.

causes.description: The dominant pathogen in most pyodermas worldwide; S. aureus produces a range of virulence factors including exfoliative toxins (responsible for bullous impetigo and scalded skin syndrome), protein A (evades opsonisation), and Panton-Valentine leukocidin (destroys neutrophils, associated with furunculosis and severe skin abscesses in CA-MRSA strains).

The primary cause of non-bullous impetigo in tropical climates and ecthyma; GAS uses surface M proteins to evade phagocytosis and produces streptolysin and hyaluronidase that facilitate spread through dermal connective tissue; untreated streptococcal pyoderma can trigger post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.

Methicillin-resistant S. aureus strains carrying the mecA gene are now common in community settings across the United States and many other countries; they disproportionately cause painful skin abscesses and recurrent furunculosis, and the USA300 clone frequently produces PVL toxin, increasing tissue necrosis.

Any breach in the epidermal barrier — wounds, insect bites, abrasions, eczema flares, surgical incisions, or skin maceration from moisture — allows organisms normally confined to the skin surface to penetrate the dermis, where they encounter a nutrient-rich environment suitable for rapid multiplication.

Diabetes mellitus impairs neutrophil chemotaxis, phagocytosis, and intracellular killing; corticosteroid therapy and biological immunosuppressants inhibit cytokine-mediated defences; HIV infection depletes CD4 lymphocytes — all creating conditions where skin pathogens establish infections that would otherwise be repelled.

S. aureus nasal carriage in 20-40% of healthy adults and household or community contact with infected individuals are key transmission routes for recurrent pyoderma; sports teams, military barracks, and childcare settings are recognised clusters for CA-MRSA outbreaks.

riskFactors.description: Poorly controlled blood glucose impairs neutrophil function and skin microcirculation; diabetics have 2- to 4-fold higher rates of skin infections, and minor wounds that would heal readily in non-diabetics can evolve into deep pyodermas or chronic infected ulcers.

Disrupted skin barrier and reduced innate antimicrobial peptide production in atopic skin permit heavy S. aureus colonisation; up to 90% of atopic dermatitis patients are colonised, and secondary impetigo and infected eczema are among the most common triggers for hospitalisation in severe disease.

Any break in the skin — cuts, abrasions, surgical incisions, insect bites, or skin picking — provides a portal of entry for pathogens; proper wound care within the first hours dramatically reduces infection risk.

Skin-fold maceration in obese individuals creates warm, moist, friction-damaged skin that is highly susceptible to both bacterial and secondary fungal pyoderma; intertrigo complicated by bacterial infection is substantially more common in those with a BMI above 35.

Corticosteroids, biological agents, chemotherapy, and HIV infection all reduce the capacity to mount an adequate innate and adaptive immune response to bacterial skin pathogens, leading to more frequent, extensive, and treatment-resistant infections.

Nasal carriage of MRSA or close contact with a carrier is the strongest predictor of recurrent community-onset MRSA skin abscesses; decolonisation protocols reduce recurrence by approximately 30-40% in household clusters.

riskFactors.factor: Warm humid climate or poor hygiene access
riskFactors.description: High ambient temperature and humidity increase skin moisture and sweating, promoting bacterial proliferation; limited access to clean water and soap in low-income settings explains the disproportionately high prevalence of pyoderma in tropical and resource-limited populations.

Children have more fragile skin, immature immune responses, and closer physical contact during play; the combination explains why impetigo and superficial pyoderma are most prevalent in the 2-6 year age group.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 2-6 years, the most affected group for impetigo and superficial pyoderma globally
Adults with diabetes mellitus or obesity, particularly for deep pyodermas, cellulitis, and abscesses
People with atopic dermatitis or other chronic skin conditions with barrier disruption
Athletes in contact sports and people in crowded residential settings (barracks, prisons, shelters)
Individuals in tropical or low-income settings, where pyoderma is among the top three childhood skin diseases
Elderly adults with reduced skin integrity, mobility limitations, and chronic oedema
diagnosisOverview: Most pyodermas are diagnosed clinically by their characteristic morphology and distribution. Impetigo is recognised by the honey-coloured crusted plaques or thin-roofed blisters on exposed areas, most often in a child. Folliculitis presents as perifollicular pustules in hair-bearing areas. A furuncle is a painful, fluctuant, erythematous nodule at a hair follicle site. Cellulitis is identified by the spreading, non-elevated erythema, warmth, and tenderness of a limb segment, typically with a clear entry point such as tinea pedis or a wound. When the diagnosis is uncertain or the infection is severe, a bacterial swab from a ruptured pustule, abscess contents, or a wound surface is sent for microscopy, culture, and antibiotic sensitivities. Swabs of intact skin or serous discharge are of limited value. In hospitalised patients or those with recurrent or treatment-resistant infection, blood cultures should be collected before antibiotics. Laboratory investigations including white cell count, CRP, and creatinine help assess severity and guide antibiotic decisions in deeper infections. Imaging — ultrasound is the first choice — is valuable when a deep abscess or necrotising infection is suspected; it distinguishes cellulitis (no pocket) from abscess (echogenic fluid collection) and helps guide incision and drainage. The key differentials for cellulitis include deep vein thrombosis, lipodermatosclerosis, contact dermatitis, erysipelas, and, importantly, necrotising fasciitis, where CT with contrast provides the best anatomical delineation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and antibiotic sensitivity profile; essential for severe, recurrent, or treatment-resistant infections and whenever MRSA is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab is applied to a ruptured pustule, the opened roof of a blister, or expressed abscess contents; results take 24-48 hours; the clinician may start empirical antibiotics before results return.

diagnosticTests.test: Incision and drainage with pus culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Both therapeutic and diagnostic for skin abscesses; releasing the pus is more important than antibiotics for fluctuant collections, and the pus is sent for culture.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under local anaesthetic; the abscess is lanced, the cavity irrigated and sometimes packed; the procedure provides immediate relief and the culture guides subsequent antibiotic choices.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes abscess from cellulitis (which lacks a fluid collection), identifies the depth and extent of collection before drainage, and detects deeper involvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied and an ultrasound probe scanned over the affected area for 5-10 minutes; it is painless and safe for all ages.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood tests (white cell count, CRP, creatinine)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies systemic inflammation and organ function in moderate to severe pyoderma; a markedly raised white count and CRP support systemic spread or deep-seated infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within a few hours; used to determine whether hospitalisation and intravenous antibiotics are needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bacteraemia — bacteria in the bloodstream — in patients with fever, haemodynamic instability, or suspected sepsis from a skin source.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets of blood cultures (aerobic and anaerobic) are collected from separate sites before starting antibiotics; results take 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects MRSA colonisation in patients with recurrent skin infections or risk factors for MRSA; guides decolonisation therapy and infection control precautions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab is taken from each nostril and any wounds; rapid PCR results are available in 1-4 hours in many laboratories.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is directed by the type and depth of pyoderma, the likely causative organism, local antibiotic resistance patterns, and the presence of risk factors for MRSA. Superficial, limited impetigo in children may be treated with topical antibiotics — fusidic acid or mupirocin applied three times daily for five to seven days achieves cure rates comparable to oral antibiotics and limits systemic exposure. Extensive impetigo, folliculitis beyond a few follicles, and all cases of furuncle, cellulitis, or suspected deep pyoderma require oral systemic antibiotics. Flucloxacillin or dicloxacillin (first-line for meticillin-susceptible S. aureus) for five to seven days achieves cure in approximately 80-90% of straightforward cellulitis and furuncle cases. When MRSA is suspected — prior MRSA infection or colonisation, failure of beta-lactam therapy, characteristic abscess in a high-prevalence community — trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, doxycycline, or clindamycin are oral alternatives. Incision and drainage is the cornerstone of treatment for skin abscesses regardless of size; antibiotics alone are insufficient to clear a fluctuant collection. A meta-analysis published in NEJM (2017) showed that trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole after drainage of MRSA skin abscesses increased the cure rate from 69% to 83%. For moderate to severe cellulitis with systemic features, intravenous co-amoxiclav, flucloxacillin, or cefazolin is given in hospital until clinical improvement allows step-down to oral therapy. Recurrent furunculosis caused by MRSA is managed with a decolonisation protocol — intranasal mupirocin ointment, chlorhexidine body washes, and household decolonisation — for five to ten days, which reduces recurrence by approximately 30-40%. Treatment of underlying conditions — improving glycaemic control, managing eczema, treating tinea pedis — is essential to prevent recurrence.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antibiotics (mupirocin, fusidic acid)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for limited impetigo; mupirocin 2% cream or ointment and fusidic acid applied three times daily for five to seven days; mupirocin is also used intranasally for S. aureus decolonisation in recurrent pyoderma.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Topical therapy achieves cure rates of 85-95% for uncomplicated impetigo, equivalent to oral antibiotics in randomised trials (Cochrane Review).

medicalTreatments.name: Flucloxacillin or dicloxacillin (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral antibiotic for meticillin-susceptible S. aureus (MSSA) pyoderma including cellulitis, furuncles, and impetigo not responding to topical treatment; given for 5-7 days for superficial infections and up to 14 days for cellulitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Oral flucloxacillin achieves clinical cure in approximately 80-90% of MSSA cellulitis cases within seven days.

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral agent for community-associated MRSA skin infections; used after incision and drainage of abscesses and for MRSA cellulitis where the organism is susceptible.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: TMP-SMX after abscess drainage increased cure rates from 69% to 83% compared with placebo in a randomised trial (Daum et al., NEJM, 2017).

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative oral antibiotic for CA-MRSA skin infections when TMP-SMX is not tolerated; active against most community MRSA strains; given twice daily for 5-7 days for abscesses and 7-14 days for cellulitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves clinical cure in approximately 70-80% of uncomplicated MRSA skin infections in outpatient settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous flucloxacillin or cefazolin
medicalTreatments.description: Used for severe MSSA cellulitis with systemic features (fever, raised inflammatory markers, rapid spread); given until clinical improvement occurs, then stepped down to oral therapy; hospital admission is required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Intravenous beta-lactam therapy resolves severe cellulitis in approximately 85-90% of MSSA cases within 72-96 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous vancomycin or daptomycin
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe or life-threatening MRSA skin infections, failed oral MRSA therapy, or bacteraemia from a skin source; dosing is guided by therapeutic drug monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Vancomycin achieves clinical cure in approximately 70-80% of complicated MRSA skin and soft tissue infections; daptomycin shows comparable efficacy in randomised trials.

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage
surgicalOptions.description: The definitive treatment for any fluctuant skin abscess regardless of causative organism; an incision is made over the pointing tip, the pus expressed and the cavity irrigated; the wound may be packed or left open to heal by secondary intention.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Incision and drainage alone achieves cure in approximately 60-70% of small abscesses from MSSA; adding antibiotic therapy increases cure rates to 80-85% for MRSA abscesses.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement for deep pyoderma
surgicalOptions.description: In ecthyma, deep necrotising infection, or pyoderma gangrenosa mimicking infection, surgical debridement removes devitalised and infected tissue; in necrotising fasciitis, early radical debridement is life-saving and must not be delayed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Early debridement in necrotising fasciitis is associated with survival rates of approximately 70-80% when performed within 24 hours; delay dramatically worsens outcomes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Warm compresses
alternativeTreatments.description: Applied three to four times daily to maturing furuncles; heat increases local blood flow and encourages spontaneous pointing and drainage without incision, appropriate for small furuncles in otherwise healthy individuals.

alternativeTreatments.name: Chlorhexidine antiseptic washes
alternativeTreatments.description: Daily whole-body washes with 4% chlorhexidine gluconate reduce cutaneous S. aureus burden and are a central component of decolonisation protocols for recurrent MRSA furunculosis.

whySeeSpecialist: Mild superficial pyoderma such as limited impetigo or a small furuncle in a healthy person is managed well by primary care. A dermatologist should be involved when infection is recurrent (three or more episodes per year), widespread, treatment-resistant, or arising in the context of an underlying skin disease such as atopic dermatitis or hidradenitis suppurativa. An infectious disease specialist adds value when MRSA is the causative organism, when bacteraemia is suspected, or when decolonisation protocols are being designed for a household cluster. Emergency surgery should be considered and consulted whenever necrotising fasciitis cannot be excluded.
doctorSelectionGuide: For uncomplicated or recurrent skin infections, choose a dermatologist with a specific interest in infectious dermatology or a general practitioner with access to wound swab and culture services. For MRSA-related disease, an infectious disease specialist with experience in decolonisation is valuable. Ensure the clinic has access to ultrasound for abscess assessment and a pathway to incision and drainage. For children with recurrent impetigo, a paediatric dermatologist who can coordinate management of any underlying atopic disease is ideal.
preventionStrategies: Clean all skin wounds promptly with soap and water, apply a topical antiseptic, and cover with a dressing to prevent bacterial entry.
Treat tinea pedis and other superficial fungal infections promptly, as they are the most common portal of entry for lower leg cellulitis.
Manage underlying conditions — optimise blood glucose control, treat eczema actively with emollients and topical steroids, and address obesity to reduce skin-fold maceration.
In households with recurrent MRSA furunculosis, all household members should undergo simultaneous decolonisation with intranasal mupirocin and chlorhexidine washes.
Avoid sharing personal items — towels, razors, sports equipment — that can transfer bacterial colonisation.
Ensure complete and correct antibiotic courses are taken to prevent recurrence and the emergence of resistance.
lifestyleModifications: Keep skin clean and dry, particularly skin folds, under breasts, and the groin, where maceration promotes infection.
Change wound dressings regularly and inspect wounds daily for early signs of infection.
Wash hands thoroughly before and after touching any infected skin area.
Wear breathable, loose-fitting clothing to reduce skin friction and moisture retention.
Trim fingernails and discourage scratching to prevent introducing bacteria from the nails into damaged skin.
Maintain well-hydrated skin with regular moisturiser to support the skin barrier, particularly important in eczema and dry-skin conditions.

recommended: A well-balanced diet supporting immune function, with adequate protein for tissue repair after skin infections
Foods rich in zinc (meat, seeds, legumes) and vitamin C (citrus, peppers) which support wound healing and immune defence
Adequate hydration to support skin barrier integrity and systemic immune function
For diabetics: a low-glycaemic-index diet with consistent carbohydrate portions to maintain blood glucose control and reduce infection susceptibility
avoid: Excess refined sugar and high-glycaemic foods in diabetics, which impair neutrophil function
Crash diets that deplete protein and micronutrients needed for skin repair and immune defence
Excessive alcohol, which impairs white cell function and skin barrier maintenance

exerciseGuidelines: Light to moderate exercise is generally safe with a small or healing pyoderma. Active skin infections that are open or draining should be covered with a waterproof dressing before any exercise to prevent spread to others. Contact sports should be avoided until infection has fully resolved and any wound is healed. Aquatic activities should be avoided until the skin is intact. After a severe cellulitis or hospitalisation for pyoderma, a gradual return to full activity over one to two weeks is appropriate once systemic signs have resolved.
prognosis: The outlook for pyoderma is excellent in otherwise healthy patients. Superficial impetigo in children typically heals without scarring within one to two weeks of appropriate treatment. Folliculitis and small furuncles resolve with incision or antibiotics in most cases. Cellulitis responds to antibiotics in the great majority of patients, with improvement expected within 48-72 hours; lack of early improvement should prompt reassessment for an abscess, an unusual organism, or a non-infectious mimic. Recurrence remains the primary long-term challenge: about 30% of patients with an initial skin abscess have a recurrence within 12 months, and recurrence rates in MRSA-positive individuals reach 50% without decolonisation. In immunocompromised patients, diabetics, and those with poor vascular supply, pyoderma can progress to deep-tissue infection, bacteraemia, and sepsis. Untreated streptococcal pyoderma in children carries a small but real risk of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis.
recoveryTimeline: Superficial impetigo treated with topical antibiotics begins to improve within 24-48 hours, with crusts resolving over five to seven days; lesions heal without scarring in most children. Folliculitis improves within three to five days of topical or systemic treatment. Furuncles drain spontaneously or after incision within 24-48 hours of treatment, and the surrounding inflammation settles over five to ten days. Cellulitis treated with antibiotics shows visible improvement — reduction in erythema and swelling — within 48-72 hours; the full antibiotic course of five to fourteen days is essential even when symptoms improve quickly. Rebound swelling and skin discolouration may persist for weeks after severe cellulitis as the lymphatic system recovers.
complications: Bacteraemia and sepsis — bacteria enter the bloodstream from any skin infection, particularly deep abscesses and severe cellulitis; risk is highest in the immunocompromised, diabetics, and the elderly.

Post-streptococcal acute glomerulonephritis — a serious kidney complication occurring one to three weeks after Group A Streptococcus skin infection, presenting with haematuria, proteinuria, hypertension, and oedema.
Recurrent infection — approximately 30% of skin abscesses recur within 12 months, rising to 50% in MRSA-positive patients without decolonisation.
Scarring and skin discolouration — deep furuncles, ecthyma, and incised abscesses frequently heal with residual scarring, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, or hypopigmentation.

Necrotising fasciitis — rare but life-threatening spread of infection to the fascia and deeper soft tissue; mortality is 20-40% even with aggressive management and is highest when diagnosis and debridement are delayed.
Lymphoedema — recurrent episodes of lower limb cellulitis damage lymphatic vessels over time, predisposing to chronic lymphoedema that itself increases the risk of future cellulitis.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Impetigo leaflet
supportResources.description: Plain-English patient information on impetigo symptoms, treatment, and how to prevent spread to others.

supportResources.name: NHS — Cellulitis
supportResources.description: NHS guidance on recognising, treating, and preventing recurrent cellulitis.

supportResources.name: CDC — Group A Streptococcal Infections
supportResources.description: CDC information on Group A Streptococcus, a key cause of pyoderma and its complications including post-streptococcal kidney disease.

sources.title: Stevens DL et al. Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections (IDSA 2014)
Daum RS et al. A Placebo-Controlled Trial of Antibiotics for Smaller Skin Abscesses. NEJM, 2017
Koning S et al. Interventions for impetigo. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2012
NICE NG141 — Cellulitis and erysipelas: antimicrobial prescribing (2019)
WHO — Skin infections: neglected tropical and endemic diseases
Hay RJ et al. The global burden of skin disease in 2010. Journal of Investigative Dermatology, 2014

confusedWithConditions.name: Erythrasma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Erythrasma is a superficial bacterial infection by Corynebacterium minutissimum producing flat, brownish, scaling patches in skin folds without pus or purulent discharge; pyoderma is characterised by pus, pustules, or abscesses.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atopic dermatitis (eczema)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema causes itchy, dry, scaly patches from immune-mediated inflammation without infection; however, infected eczema (secondary pyoderma) shows weeping, crusting, and honey-coloured plaques superimposed on eczematous skin.

confusedWithConditions.name: Erysipelas
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Erysipelas is a superficial dermal Streptococcal infection with a sharply demarcated, raised, bright-red border; cellulitis has blurred margins and is deeper. Both are pyodermas but erysipelas is clinically distinct and treated similarly.

relatedConditions.name: Impetigo
relatedConditions.relevance: Impetigo is the most common superficial pyoderma; its honey-coloured crusted plaques are caused by the same staphylococcal and streptococcal pathogens and are managed with topical or oral antibiotics depending on severity.

relatedConditions.name: Erysipelas
relatedConditions.relevance: Erysipelas is a Group A Streptococcus skin infection sharing the same bacterial family as common pyodermas; it presents with a distinctive well-demarcated raised red patch and is treated with penicillin-class antibiotics.

relatedConditions.name: Erythrasma
relatedConditions.relevance: Erythrasma is a superficial bacterial skin infection caused by Corynebacterium minutissimum, clinically distinct from pyoderma but sharing the same skin-fold distribution and sometimes coexisting with pyoderma.

relatedConditions.name: Acne vulgaris
relatedConditions.relevance: Acne involves superficial follicular inflammation that can become secondarily infected with S. aureus, blurring the boundary between comedonal acne and pyoderma of the follicle (folliculitis).

faqs.question: What is pyoderma?
faqs.answer: Pyoderma means a bacterial skin infection that produces pus. It ranges from superficial infections like impetigo and folliculitis to deeper ones like cellulitis and skin abscesses. The two most common causes are Staphylococcus aureus and Group A Streptococcus.

faqs.question: Is pyoderma contagious?
faqs.answer: Yes. Superficial pyoderma such as impetigo is highly contagious and spreads easily by direct contact with infected skin or contaminated surfaces. Infected lesions should be covered, and household members should avoid sharing towels and wash hands frequently until the infection resolves.

faqs.question: What does pyoderma look like?
faqs.answer: Pyoderma appears as pus-filled spots, blisters, or open sores on the skin. Impetigo causes honey-coloured crusts, usually around the nose and mouth. Folliculitis produces small pustules at hair follicles. Abscesses form as painful, swollen, red lumps. Cellulitis shows spreading redness and warmth without a defined pus pocket.

faqs.question: What antibiotic is used for pyoderma?
faqs.answer: Limited impetigo is treated with topical mupirocin or fusidic acid. Deeper infections and widespread impetigo use oral flucloxacillin or dicloxacillin for meticillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus. If MRSA is suspected, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or doxycycline is used. Severe infections require intravenous antibiotics in hospital.

faqs.question: Can pyoderma go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Minor superficial pyoderma such as a single small folliculitis pustule or an early furuncle may resolve without treatment in a healthy person. However, impetigo rarely clears without antibiotics and spreads rapidly, and abscesses require drainage. Delay in treatment risks deeper spread. Medical review is always recommended.

faqs.question: How long does pyoderma last?
faqs.answer: With appropriate treatment, superficial impetigo clears within five to seven days. Folliculitis improves in three to five days. A skin abscess after drainage heals within one to two weeks. Cellulitis requires five to fourteen days of antibiotics, with visible improvement expected within 48-72 hours. Without treatment, pyoderma worsens and spreads.

faqs.question: Is pyoderma the same as impetigo?
faqs.answer: Impetigo is one type of pyoderma. Pyoderma is the broader term covering all pus-forming bacterial skin infections, including impetigo, folliculitis, furuncles, cellulitis, and abscesses. Impetigo is the most common superficial form, particularly in children.

faqs.question: What causes recurrent pyoderma?
faqs.answer: Recurrent pyoderma is most often caused by persistent nasal or skin carriage of Staphylococcus aureus, including MRSA. Other contributors include atopic dermatitis, diabetes, obesity, poor hygiene, and repeated skin trauma. A decolonisation protocol with nasal mupirocin and chlorhexidine washes reduces recurrence by 30-40%.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause skin infections?
faqs.answer: Yes. People with diabetes are 2- to 4-fold more likely to develop skin infections including pyoderma because high blood glucose impairs neutrophil function, reduces blood supply to the skin, and slows wound healing. Poorly controlled diabetes makes pyoderma more frequent, deeper, and harder to treat.

faqs.question: How do I prevent pyoderma from spreading?
faqs.answer: Keep infected areas covered with clean dressings. Wash hands thoroughly before and after touching the infection. Avoid sharing towels, clothing, or sports equipment. Bathe daily and keep skin clean and dry. Start treatment promptly and complete the full antibiotic course to prevent spreading and recurrence.

faqs.question: Does eczema increase the risk of pyoderma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Atopic dermatitis breaks down the skin barrier and reduces natural antimicrobial peptides, allowing Staphylococcus aureus to colonise up to 90% of affected skin. Secondary bacterial infection (impetiginised eczema) is one of the most common complications of atopic dermatitis and often requires a course of oral antibiotics.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cellulitis and an abscess?
faqs.answer: Cellulitis is a diffuse spreading infection of the skin and tissues without a defined pus-filled cavity; it appears as a warm, red, swollen area that feels solid. An abscess is a walled-off collection of pus that forms a fluctuant, painful lump. Abscesses require incision and drainage; cellulitis is treated with antibiotics.

faqs.question: When is pyoderma an emergency?
faqs.answer: Call emergency services if the red area spreads rapidly over hours, if there is severe pain or paradoxically the skin becomes numb, if blistering or skin discolouration appears, or if fever, confusion, or a rapid pulse develops. These signs may indicate necrotising fasciitis or sepsis, both of which are life-threatening.

faqs.question: Can pyoderma cause kidney problems?
faqs.answer: Yes, in children. Skin infection with Group A Streptococcus can trigger post-streptococcal acute glomerulonephritis one to three weeks after the skin infection, causing blood in the urine, protein in the urine, swelling, and raised blood pressure. It is most common in children aged 5-12 in tropical regions where streptococcal pyoderma is prevalent.

faqs.question: What is MRSA pyoderma?
faqs.answer: MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) pyoderma is a skin infection caused by a resistant strain of Staphylococcus that does not respond to standard penicillin-type antibiotics. Community MRSA typically causes painful, rapidly enlarging abscesses. Treatment requires drainage and specific antibiotics such as trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or doxycycline.

faqs.question: Should I pop a skin boil or abscess at home?
faqs.answer: No. Squeezing or lancing a boil at home risks pushing bacteria deeper into the tissue, spreading infection, and introducing new bacteria. A doctor or nurse can safely incise and drain the abscess with proper technique and sterile equipment. Most abscesses are managed as an outpatient procedure under local anaesthetic.

faqs.question: How is pyoderma treated in children?
faqs.answer: Limited impetigo in children is treated with topical mupirocin or fusidic acid applied three times daily for five to seven days. Widespread impetigo, or impetigo that does not clear with topical therapy, is treated with oral cefalexin or flucloxacillin. Children should stay home from school or nursery until lesions are crusted over or 48 hours after starting antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can pyoderma affect the face?
faqs.answer: Yes. The face — particularly the area around the nose and mouth — is the most common site for impetigo, especially in children. Folliculitis affects the beard area in adults who shave. Facial cellulitis around the eye (periorbital cellulitis) requires urgent assessment because infection can spread into the orbit, which is a serious and potentially vision-threatening complication.

faqs.question: What is decolonisation and who needs it?
faqs.answer: Decolonisation means eliminating MRSA or S. aureus from the nose and skin to prevent recurrent infection. It involves applying mupirocin antibiotic ointment inside both nostrils twice daily for five days and washing the whole body with chlorhexidine antiseptic for five days. It is recommended for people who have had three or more skin infections in a year, and ideally for all household members at the same time.

faqs.question: Does pyoderma leave scars?
faqs.answer: Superficial impetigo usually heals without scarring, though temporary redness or pigmentation may persist. Deep furuncles, ecthyma, and incised abscesses frequently heal with a visible scar, particularly in darker skin tones where post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation can be pronounced. Early and effective treatment reduces the depth of tissue destruction and the likelihood of permanent scarring.

metaDescription: Pyoderma is a bacterial skin infection causing pus, redness, and swelling from staph or strep bacteria. Learn types, antibiotic treatment, and prevention.
